name: Network Access
description: Handles physical transmission of bits and framing/addressing for delivery on the local network segment. Combines what OSI splits into Physical and Data Link — the TCP/IP model doesn't care how bits move, only that the underlying medium can carry an IP packet end-to-end on a single hop.

fields.name: Destination MAC
fields.description: Hardware address of the receiving NIC on this segment. ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff is broadcast.

fields.name: Source MAC
fields.description: Hardware address of the sending NIC. Trivially spoofable.

fields.name: EtherType
fields.description: Identifies the encapsulated protocol — 0x0800 IPv4, 0x0806 ARP, 0x86DD IPv6, 0x8100 802.1Q VLAN tag.

fields.name: Payload
fields.description: The encapsulated packet — typically an IP datagram. Padded to 46 bytes minimum.

fields.name: FCS
fields.description: Frame Check Sequence — CRC-32 over header + payload for error detection. Stripped by most capture tools.

name: Internet
description: Moves packets between hosts across interconnected networks using logical addresses (IP). Decides routes, fragments oversized datagrams, and provides best-effort delivery. The 'IP' in TCP/IP — the layer that makes a single global internet possible.
examples: Routing protocols — BGP, OSPF, RIP, EIGRP

fields.name: Version
fields.description: IP version — always 4 for IPv4.

fields.name: IHL
fields.description: Internet Header Length in 32-bit words. Usually 5 (= 20 bytes); larger when options are present.

fields.name: DSCP / ECN
fields.description: Differentiated Services Code Point + Explicit Congestion Notification — QoS marking and congestion signalling.

fields.name: Total Length
fields.description: Length of the entire IP packet (header + data) in bytes. Max 65,535.

fields.name: Identification
fields.description: Unique ID per packet, used to reassemble fragments belonging to the same datagram.

fields.name: Flags + Frag Offset
fields.description: DF (Don't Fragment), MF (More Fragments), and the fragment's position in the original datagram.

fields.name: TTL
fields.description: Hop limit — decremented by each router. Reaches 0 → packet dropped, ICMP Time Exceeded sent. The mechanism behind traceroute.

fields.name: Protocol
fields.description: Identifies the next-layer protocol — 6 TCP, 17 UDP, 1 ICMP, 50 ESP, 51 AH.

fields.name: Header Checksum
fields.description: Checksum over the IP header only (not data). Recomputed at every hop because TTL changes.

fields.name: Source IP
fields.description: Sender's IPv4 address — the return address.

fields.name: Destination IP
fields.description: Recipient's IPv4 address — what routers use for forwarding decisions.

fields.name: Options
fields.description: Rare extensions — Record Route, Loose/Strict Source Routing, Timestamp. Often blocked by firewalls as a security risk.

name: Transport
description: End-to-end delivery between processes on hosts, identified by port numbers. Adds reliability, ordering, and flow control where needed (TCP) or stays out of the way for speed (UDP). The boundary between 'the network' and 'the application'.
examples: TCP — reliable, connection-oriented, ordered byte stream
SCTP — multi-streaming, used in telecom signalling
Port numbers (0–65535) and the TCP three-way handshake

fields.name: Source Port
fields.description: Sender's port — usually an ephemeral port chosen by the OS.

fields.name: Destination Port
fields.description: Receiver's port — identifies the listening service (80 HTTP, 443 HTTPS, 22 SSH).

fields.name: Sequence Number
fields.description: Byte offset of this segment's first data byte within the overall stream. Initialised randomly per connection.

fields.name: Ack Number
fields.description: Next sequence number the sender expects to receive. Acknowledges everything before it.

fields.name: Data Offset
fields.description: TCP header length in 32-bit words. 5 = 20 bytes; larger when options are present.

fields.name: Flags
fields.description: Control bits — URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN, plus ECN signalling. SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK is the three-way handshake.

fields.name: Window
fields.description: Receive window size — how many more bytes the sender is willing to accept. The basis of TCP flow control.

fields.name: Checksum
fields.description: Checksum over the TCP header + data + an IP pseudo-header. Mandatory.

fields.name: Urgent Pointer
fields.description: Offset of urgent data when the URG flag is set. Rarely used in practice.

fields.name: Options
fields.description: MSS (Max Segment Size), window scaling, SACK (Selective Acknowledgement), timestamps — negotiated during the handshake.

fields.name: Source Port
fields.description: Sender's port. Optional in IPv4 (may be 0); mandatory in IPv6.

fields.name: Destination Port
fields.description: Receiver's port — identifies the listening service (53 DNS, 123 NTP, 161 SNMP).

fields.name: Length
fields.description: Length of the UDP header + data in bytes (minimum 8).

fields.name: Checksum
fields.description: Checksum over header + data + IP pseudo-header. Optional in IPv4 (0 = not used); mandatory in IPv6.

name: Application
description: Everything above transport — the protocols and message formats applications speak directly. TCP/IP collapses session management, data representation, and the user-facing protocol into a single layer; in practice each application protocol handles whichever of those concerns it needs.
examples: HTTP / HTTPS, DNS, SMTP, IMAP, POP3
TLS / SSL — encryption and integrity for application data
SNMP, LDAP, Kerberos, NTP, RDP, SMB

headers.name: HTTP/1.1 Request (representative)
headers.size: Variable — text-based, terminated by a blank line

fields.name: Request line
fields.description: Method + path + version, e.g. GET /index.html HTTP/1.1

fields.name: Host
fields.description: Required since HTTP/1.1 — names the virtual host being requested. Enables many sites on one IP.

fields.name: User-Agent
fields.description: Identifies the client software (browser, curl, library). Often used for fingerprinting.

fields.name: Accept
fields.description: Media types the client is willing to receive (text/html, application/json).

fields.name: Cookie
fields.description: Stored session/state data echoed back to the server. Authentication tokens often live here.

fields.name: Authorization
fields.description: Credentials — Basic, Bearer (JWT/OAuth), Digest. Sensitive; never log raw values.

fields.name: Content-Type
fields.description: Media type of the request body (application/json, multipart/form-data) when present.

fields.name: Content-Length
fields.description: Size of the request body in bytes. Mismatch with actual body is a classic request-smuggling vector.

fields.name: Body
fields.description: Optional payload — JSON, form data, file upload. Separated from headers by a blank line.

fields.name: No fixed format
fields.description: There's no single 'application header' — every protocol defines its own message format. Some are text (HTTP, SMTP), some are binary (DNS, SSH, TLS records). HTTP shown as the canonical example.
